wrong_explanations.3: The higher melting point comes from the higher ionic charge (O²⁻), not simply from more atoms.

quiz.q: Explain why the melting point of an ionic compound gives information about the strength of the forces between its ions.
opts: A higher melting point means more energy is needed to separate the ions, which shows the electrostatic forces holding them are stronger
A higher melting point means the ions are lighter
The melting point depends only on the size of the crystal
The melting point is unrelated to the forces between ions

wrong_explanations.1: Ion mass is not what the melting point reveals; it reflects the strength of the attraction.
wrong_explanations.2: Crystal size does not set the melting point; the strength of the ionic forces does.
wrong_explanations.3: The melting point is directly linked to the strength of the forces between the ions.

quiz.q: Suggest why magnesium oxide is used to line the inside of furnaces.
opts: Its 2+/2− ions give very strong ionic bonding and a very high melting point, so it can withstand the high temperatures without melting
It is a good electrical conductor when solid
It has a low melting point, so it melts to seal the furnace
It is a soft, flexible material that absorbs heat

wrong_explanations.1: Solid MgO does not conduct electricity (fixed ions); that is not why it is used to line furnaces.
wrong_explanations.2: A low melting point would be useless in a furnace; MgO is chosen for its VERY HIGH melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: MgO is hard and brittle, not soft and flexible; it is chosen for heat resistance.

quiz.q: Aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) has a very high melting point. Explain, in terms of ionic charge, why it is higher than that of sodium chloride.
opts: Al³⁺ and O²⁻ carry high charges (3+ and 2−), giving very strong electrostatic forces that need a great deal of energy to overcome — far more than NaCl's 1+/1−
Aluminium oxide has smaller ions, which always melt at higher temperatures
Aluminium oxide is a covalent compound with strong bonds
Aluminium is a metal, so aluminium oxide conducts and melts easily

wrong_explanations.1: Ion size has a smaller effect than charge; the 3+/2− charges are the main reason for the very high melting point.
wrong_explanations.2: Aluminium oxide is ionic (metal + non-metal), not covalent.
wrong_explanations.3: Aluminium oxide is an ionic compound with a very HIGH melting point, and it does not conduct as a solid.

quiz.q: Explain why the ability of a molten ionic compound to conduct electricity depends on its ions acting as charge carriers.
opts: A current is a flow of charge; in a molten ionic compound the mobile ions carry that charge, so conduction only happens when the ions are free to move
The ions carry charge only when they are fixed in place
Electrons in the ions carry the charge whether the compound is solid or molten
The compound conducts because heat, not moving charge, carries the current

wrong_explanations.1: Fixed ions cannot carry charge; they must be free to move, which happens when the compound is molten.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic conduction is by moving ions, not by electrons, and only when the ions can move.
wrong_explanations.3: Electrical current is a flow of charge (moving ions here), not simply heat.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say that ionic solutions conduct electricity because electrons flow through them, just like in a metal. They do not: ionic compounds have no free electrons. They conduct only when molten or dissolved, and it is the ions themselves that move and carry the charge. (This is also why ionic compounds are brittle rather than bendy — shifting the lattice brings like charges together, and they repel.)

bonding/properties-small-molecules.summary: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting points and do not conduct electricity.

quiz.q: Water (H₂O) boils at 100°C but methane (CH₄) boils at −161°C, although both are simple molecular. Explain why the difference is so large.
opts: Water molecules have stronger intermolecular forces than methane molecules, so more energy is needed to separate them
Water has more covalent bonds, so it takes more energy to break them
Methane is lighter, so it simply evaporates faster
Water contains oxygen, which makes the molecules too heavy to boil easily

wrong_explanations.1: Boiling does not break covalent bonds — water has 2 and methane has 4, so bond count is not the reason.
wrong_explanations.2: Lighter molecules can evaporate faster, but boiling point is set by the strength of the intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.3: The key reason is the stronger intermolecular forces in water, not simply the mass of the molecules.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances do not conduct electricity.
opts: They have no free electrons or ions — there are no charged particles that can move to carry a current
Their covalent bonds are too strong to let electrons move
Their molecules are too small to hold a charge
Simple molecules are always gases, and gases never conduct

wrong_explanations.1: Conduction needs free charged particles, not weaker bonds — even weak bonds would not help without free charges.
wrong_explanations.2: Molecular size is not the point; metals are large structures and conduct because of free electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Some gases can conduct (e.g. when ionised), and simple molecular substances can be solids or liquids too.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting and boiling points.
opts: The forces between the molecules (intermolecular forces) are weak, so only a little energy is needed to separate them
The covalent bonds within the molecules are very weak
The molecules lose electrons easily when warmed
There are no forces at all between the molecules

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds within molecules are strong; it is the forces between molecules that are weak.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are lost on melting; the weak intermolecular forces are simply overcome.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE forces between molecules (intermolecular forces) — they are just weak.

quiz.q: State what is overcome when a simple molecular substance boils.
opts: The weak intermolecular forces between the molecules
The strong covalent bonds within the molecules
The ionic bonds between the molecules
The metallic bonds holding the molecules together

wrong_explanations.1: The strong covalent bonds inside the molecules are NOT broken on boiling.
wrong_explanations.2: Simple molecular substances contain no ionic bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no metallic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

quiz.q: Describe what a simple molecular substance is.
opts: A substance made of small molecules, each with a fixed number of atoms joined by strong covalent bonds
A giant lattice of ions held by electrostatic forces
A giant structure of atoms all joined by covalent bonds
A metal made of positive ions in a sea of electrons

wrong_explanations.1: A giant ionic lattice describes an ionic compound, not a simple molecular substance.
wrong_explanations.2: A giant covalent structure (like diamond) is different — its covalent bonds run throughout, with no small molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes a metal, not a molecular substance.

quiz.q: State whether simple molecular substances have high or low melting points.
opts: Low
High
Always exactly 100°C
They never melt

wrong_explanations.1: Simple molecular substances have LOW melting points because of weak intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.2: 100°C is the boiling point of water specifically, not a general rule.
wrong_explanations.3: They do melt — easily, in fact, at low temperatures.

quiz.q: State whether simple molecular substances conduct electricity.
opts: No
Yes, always
Only when solid
Only as a metal

wrong_explanations.1: They have no free charged particles, so they do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.2: They do not conduct in any state (no free electrons or ions).
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances are not metals and do not conduct.

quiz.q: Name the type of force that must be overcome to melt a simple molecular substance.
opts: Intermolecular forces
Covalent bonds
Ionic bonds
Metallic bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds are within molecules and are not broken on melting.
wrong_explanations.2: There are no ionic bonds in a simple molecular substance.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no metallic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

quiz.q: Identify which of these is a simple molecular substance.
opts: Carbon dioxide
Sodium chloride
Copper
Diamond

wrong_explanations.1: Sodium chloride is a giant ionic lattice, not a simple molecule.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is a metal with a giant metallic structure.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond is a giant covalent structure, not a simple molecule.

quiz.q: State whether the covalent bonds inside the molecules break when a simple molecular substance melts.
opts: No — only the forces between the molecules are overcome
Yes — the covalent bonds break to let it melt
Yes — half of the covalent bonds break
Only if the substance is heated very slowly

wrong_explanations.1: The strong covalent bonds stay intact; melting overcomes the weak forces between molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: No covalent bonds break on melting, not even some of them.
wrong_explanations.3: The heating rate does not change which forces are overcome; the covalent bonds stay intact.

quiz.q: State the two reasons why simple molecular substances do not conduct electricity.
opts: They have no free electrons and no free ions
They have strong covalent bonds and are solid
They are large and heavy
They have delocalised electrons that are fixed in place

wrong_explanations.1: Bond strength and state are not the reason — the absence of free charged particles is.
wrong_explanations.2: Size and mass do not determine conductivity.
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances have no delocalised electrons at all.

quiz.q: Predict the physical state of most simple molecular substances at room temperature.
opts: Gases or liquids (with some low-melting solids)
Always solids with very high melting points
Always metals
Always ionic crystals

wrong_explanations.1: Their weak intermolecular forces give low melting and boiling points, so many are gases or liquids.
wrong_explanations.2: High-melting solids describe giant structures, not simple molecular substances.
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances are non-metals; they are not metals or ionic crystals.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say that simple molecular substances melt easily because their covalent bonds are weak. The covalent bonds inside each molecule are actually strong. What is weak are the intermolecular forces BETWEEN the separate molecules — melting or boiling overcomes those and leaves the covalent bonds untouched, which is why the melting point is low.

quiz.q: Water (H₂O) boils at 100°C but methane (CH₄) boils at −161°C, although both are simple molecular. Explain why the difference is so large.
opts: Water molecules have stronger intermolecular forces than methane molecules, so more energy is needed to separate them
Water has more covalent bonds, so it takes more energy to break them
Methane is lighter, so it simply evaporates faster
Water contains oxygen, which makes the molecules too heavy to boil easily

wrong_explanations.1: Boiling does not break covalent bonds — water has 2 and methane has 4, so bond count is not the reason.
wrong_explanations.2: Lighter molecules can evaporate faster, but boiling point is set by the strength of the intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.3: The key reason is the stronger intermolecular forces in water, not simply the mass of the molecules.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances do not conduct electricity.
opts: They have no free electrons or ions — there are no charged particles that can move to carry a current
Their covalent bonds are too strong to let electrons move
Their molecules are too small to hold a charge
Simple molecules are always gases, and gases never conduct

wrong_explanations.1: Conduction needs free charged particles, not weaker bonds — even weak bonds would not help without free charges.
wrong_explanations.2: Molecular size is not the point; metals are large structures and conduct because of free electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Some gases can conduct (e.g. when ionised), and simple molecular substances can be solids or liquids too.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting and boiling points.
opts: The forces between the molecules (intermolecular forces) are weak, so only a little energy is needed to separate them
The covalent bonds within the molecules are very weak
The molecules lose electrons easily when warmed
There are no forces at all between the molecules

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds within molecules are strong; it is the forces between molecules that are weak.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are lost on melting; the weak intermolecular forces are simply overcome.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE forces between molecules (intermolecular forces) — they are just weak.

quiz.q: State what is overcome when a simple molecular substance boils.
opts: The weak intermolecular forces between the molecules
The strong covalent bonds within the molecules
The ionic bonds between the molecules
The metallic bonds holding the molecules together

wrong_explanations.1: The strong covalent bonds inside the molecules are NOT broken on boiling.
wrong_explanations.2: Simple molecular substances contain no ionic bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no metallic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

quiz.q: Describe what a simple molecular substance is.
opts: A substance made of small molecules, each with a fixed number of atoms joined by strong covalent bonds
A giant lattice of ions held by electrostatic forces
A giant structure of atoms all joined by covalent bonds
A metal made of positive ions in a sea of electrons

wrong_explanations.1: A giant ionic lattice describes an ionic compound, not a simple molecular substance.
wrong_explanations.2: A giant covalent structure (like diamond) is different — its covalent bonds run throughout, with no small molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes a metal, not a molecular substance.

quiz.q: Explain why larger molecules generally have higher melting and boiling points than smaller molecules.
opts: Larger molecules have more electrons, giving stronger intermolecular forces, so more energy is needed to separate them
Larger molecules have stronger covalent bonds inside them
Larger molecules are always ionic
Larger molecules contain free electrons that raise the melting point

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds inside are not what melts; it is the intermolecular forces, which grow with molecule size.
wrong_explanations.2: Larger molecular substances are still covalent/molecular, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances have no free electrons; the higher melting point is due to stronger intermolecular forces.

quiz.q: Explain why melting a simple molecular substance does not break its covalent bonds.
opts: Melting only needs enough energy to overcome the weak intermolecular forces between molecules, which is far less than the energy needed to break the strong covalent bonds
The covalent bonds are weaker than the intermolecular forces
The molecules gain electrons that protect the covalent bonds
The covalent bonds are shared out among all the molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds are much STRONGER than intermolecular forces, so melting overcomes the weak forces only.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are gained on melting.
wrong_explanations.3: Covalent bonds stay within each molecule; they are not shared between molecules.

quiz.q: Many simple molecular substances are gases at room temperature, but iodine is a solid. Suggest why iodine is a solid.
opts: Iodine molecules are relatively large, so the intermolecular forces between them are strong enough to hold them as a solid at room temperature
Iodine has stronger covalent bonds than other molecules
Iodine is an ionic compound
Iodine molecules contain free electrons

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bond strength inside I₂ is not the reason; it is the stronger intermolecular forces between larger molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: Iodine (I₂) is a simple molecular substance, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.3: Iodine has no free electrons; its solid state is due to intermolecular forces.

quiz.q: A substance has a low melting point and does not conduct electricity in any state. Deduce its structure and bonding.
opts: Simple molecular, with covalent bonding — low melting point (weak intermolecular forces) and no charged particles to conduct
Ionic — it has a low melting point and free ions
Metallic — it does not conduct because the electrons are fixed
Giant covalent — low melting point from weak covalent bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds have HIGH melting points and conduct when molten — this does neither.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals conduct (delocalised electrons) and usually have high melting points.
wrong_explanations.3: Giant covalent structures have very HIGH melting points, not low ones.

quiz.q: Explain why the melting point of a simple molecular substance tells you nothing about the strength of its covalent bonds.
opts: Melting overcomes only the weak intermolecular forces between molecules; the strong covalent bonds inside the molecules are not involved
Because the covalent bonds break at exactly the melting point
Because covalent bonds have no strength to measure
Because the melting point measures the ionic bonds instead

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds are not broken on melting, so the melting point cannot tell you their strength.
wrong_explanations.2: Covalent bonds are strong and definitely have a measurable strength — just not one shown by the melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no ionic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

quiz.q: Chlorine (Cl₂) is a gas at room temperature but iodine (I₂) is a solid, even though both are simple molecular. Explain this difference.
opts: Iodine molecules are larger (more electrons), so the intermolecular forces between them are stronger and hold them as a solid, whereas chlorine's weaker forces let it be a gas
Iodine has stronger covalent bonds than chlorine
Iodine is ionic while chlorine is covalent
Chlorine molecules are heavier than iodine molecules

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bond inside each molecule is not what melts; it is the intermolecular forces, stronger for larger iodine.
wrong_explanations.2: Both are simple covalent molecules; neither is ionic.
wrong_explanations.3: Iodine molecules are actually heavier/larger than chlorine — that is why their intermolecular forces are stronger.

quiz.q: Explain one limitation of representing a simple molecular substance by drawing just a single molecule.
opts: A single molecule does not show that the substance contains many molecules with intermolecular forces between them — which is what actually controls its melting and boiling points
A single molecule shows the wrong number of atoms
A single molecule wrongly shows ionic bonding
A single molecule cannot show the covalent bonds

wrong_explanations.1: A correct single-molecule diagram shows the right atoms — that is not the limitation.
wrong_explanations.2: A molecular diagram shows covalent bonding, not ionic; the limitation is about the forces between molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: A single molecule CAN show its covalent bonds; what it cannot show is the forces between separate molecules.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say that simple molecular substances melt easily because their covalent bonds are weak. The covalent bonds inside each molecule are actually strong. What is weak are the intermolecular forces BETWEEN the separate molecules — melting or boiling overcomes those and leaves the covalent bonds untouched, which is why the melting point is low.

bonding/polymers.summary: Describe the structure of polymers and explain their properties.

quiz.q: Explain why polymers have much lower melting points than giant covalent structures such as diamond.
opts: A polymer is made of separate large molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces, while diamond has strong covalent bonds throughout the whole structure
Polymers contain fewer atoms than diamond, so there is less to heat
Diamond has ionic bonds, which are stronger than the covalent bonds in polymers
Polymers are man-made, and natural structures are always stronger

wrong_explanations.1: A single polymer chain can contain millions of atoms — the difference is the TYPE of forces, not the number of atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent, with no ionic bonds; its strong covalent network gives the high melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: Whether a material is natural or synthetic does not set its melting point; structure and bonding do.

quiz.q: Ethene (CH₂=CH₂) can be used to make poly(ethene), but ethane (C₂H₆) cannot. Explain why.
opts: Ethene has a C=C double bond that can open up so the monomers join together; ethane is saturated (only single bonds), so it cannot join in this way
Ethane is too heavy a molecule to react
Ethane contains no carbon, so it cannot form a carbon chain
Ethene is an ionic compound, which is needed for polymerisation

wrong_explanations.1: Molecular mass is not the barrier; ethane simply has no C=C double bond to open up.
wrong_explanations.2: Ethane (C₂H₆) does contain carbon — it just has no double bond to enable addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: Ethene is a covalent molecule, not ionic; the double bond is what matters.

quiz.q: Describe how a polymer forms from monomers.
opts: Many small monomer molecules join together to form one long chain molecule
A single large molecule breaks apart into many small ones
Two atoms share electrons to form one molecule
Positive and negative ions attract to form a lattice

wrong_explanations.1: Polymerisation joins monomers together; it does not break a molecule apart.
wrong_explanations.2: A single shared pair between two atoms is one covalent bond, not polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes ionic bonding, not the formation of a polymer.

quiz.q: Explain why poly(ethene) is a solid at room temperature.
opts: Its molecules are very long chains, so there is enough intermolecular force between them to hold it as a solid
Its covalent bonds are ionic and therefore strong
It contains a giant lattice of ions
It has delocalised electrons that lock it in place

wrong_explanations.1: Poly(ethene) has covalent, not ionic, bonds; its solid state is due to intermolecular forces between long chains.
wrong_explanations.2: Poly(ethene) is molecular, not a giant ionic lattice.
wrong_explanations.3: Poly(ethene) has no delocalised electrons; it is an insulator.

quiz.q: State what is meant by a monomer.
opts: A small molecule that can join with many others to form a polymer
The long chain molecule made when many small molecules join
A single atom that makes up a polymer
A mixture of two different plastics

wrong_explanations.1: The long chain is the POLYMER; the monomer is the small starting molecule.
wrong_explanations.2: A monomer is a molecule, not a single atom.
wrong_explanations.3: A monomer is a pure substance (one type of molecule), not a mixture.

quiz.q: State what a polymer is.
opts: A very large molecule made of many repeating units
A small molecule with only a few atoms
A giant lattice of ions
A pure metal

wrong_explanations.1: Polymers are large, not small, molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymers are molecular, not ionic lattices.
wrong_explanations.3: Polymers are non-metals, not metals.

quiz.q: Name the small molecules that join together to make a polymer.
opts: Monomers
Polymers
Isotopes
Alloys

wrong_explanations.1: The polymer is the final long chain; the small starting molecules are monomers.
wrong_explanations.2: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons — unrelated.
wrong_explanations.3: An alloy is a mixture of metals, not a building block of a polymer.

quiz.q: State whether polymers conduct electricity.
opts: No — they are insulators
Yes — like metals
Only when solid
Only when molten

wrong_explanations.1: Polymers have no free electrons or ions, so they are insulators.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymers do not conduct in any state — they have no free charged particles.
wrong_explanations.3: Melting a polymer does not give it free charged particles, so it still does not conduct.

quiz.q: Identify the monomer used to make poly(ethene).
opts: Ethene
Ethane
Ethanol
Methane

wrong_explanations.1: Ethane is saturated (no C=C), so it cannot undergo addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.2: Ethanol is an alcohol and does not form poly(ethene).
wrong_explanations.3: Methane has only one carbon and no double bond, so it cannot form poly(ethene).

quiz.q: State the type of bond in a monomer that allows addition polymerisation to happen.
opts: A carbon–carbon double bond (C=C)
A single carbon–carbon bond
An ionic bond
A metallic bond

wrong_explanations.1: A single C–C bond cannot open up to join monomers; a C=C double bond is needed.
wrong_explanations.2: Monomers are covalent molecules, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.3: Monomers contain no metallic bonding.

quiz.q: Identify an everyday use of poly(ethene).
opts: Plastic bags and bottles
Electrical wiring cores
Window glass
Saucepan bases

wrong_explanations.1: Electrical wiring cores are made from copper (a conductor), not poly(ethene).
wrong_explanations.2: Window glass is not a polymer.
wrong_explanations.3: Saucepan bases are metal (good heat conductors), not poly(ethene).

quiz.q: Predict whether a polymer will conduct electricity, and give a reason.
opts: No — it has no free electrons or ions to carry a charge
Yes — its long chains carry the current
Yes — because it contains carbon
Only when dissolved in water

wrong_explanations.1: Chain length does not give free charged particles, so a polymer does not conduct.
wrong_explanations.2: Containing carbon does not make a substance a conductor (diamond is carbon and does not conduct).
wrong_explanations.3: Polymers do not release ions when in contact with water; they remain insulators.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often call polymers giant covalent structures because the chains are so large. They are not: a polymer is a very large molecule, and the chains are held to each other only by weak intermolecular forces. In a giant covalent structure such as diamond, strong covalent bonds run all the way through — which is why diamond's melting point is far higher than any polymer's.

quiz.q: Explain why polymers have much lower melting points than giant covalent structures such as diamond.
opts: A polymer is made of separate large molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces, while diamond has strong covalent bonds throughout the whole structure
Polymers contain fewer atoms than diamond, so there is less to heat
Diamond has ionic bonds, which are stronger than the covalent bonds in polymers
Polymers are man-made, and natural structures are always stronger

wrong_explanations.1: A single polymer chain can contain millions of atoms — the difference is the TYPE of forces, not the number of atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent, with no ionic bonds; its strong covalent network gives the high melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: Whether a material is natural or synthetic does not set its melting point; structure and bonding do.

quiz.q: Ethene (CH₂=CH₂) can be used to make poly(ethene), but ethane (C₂H₆) cannot. Explain why.
opts: Ethene has a C=C double bond that can open up so the monomers join together; ethane is saturated (only single bonds), so it cannot join in this way
Ethane is too heavy a molecule to react
Ethane contains no carbon, so it cannot form a carbon chain
Ethene is an ionic compound, which is needed for polymerisation

wrong_explanations.1: Molecular mass is not the barrier; ethane simply has no C=C double bond to open up.
wrong_explanations.2: Ethane (C₂H₆) does contain carbon — it just has no double bond to enable addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: Ethene is a covalent molecule, not ionic; the double bond is what matters.

quiz.q: Describe how a polymer forms from monomers.
opts: Many small monomer molecules join together to form one long chain molecule
A single large molecule breaks apart into many small ones
Two atoms share electrons to form one molecule
Positive and negative ions attract to form a lattice

wrong_explanations.1: Polymerisation joins monomers together; it does not break a molecule apart.
wrong_explanations.2: A single shared pair between two atoms is one covalent bond, not polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes ionic bonding, not the formation of a polymer.

quiz.q: Explain why poly(ethene) is a solid at room temperature.
opts: Its molecules are very long chains, so there is enough intermolecular force between them to hold it as a solid
Its covalent bonds are ionic and therefore strong
It contains a giant lattice of ions
It has delocalised electrons that lock it in place

wrong_explanations.1: Poly(ethene) has covalent, not ionic, bonds; its solid state is due to intermolecular forces between long chains.
wrong_explanations.2: Poly(ethene) is molecular, not a giant ionic lattice.
wrong_explanations.3: Poly(ethene) has no delocalised electrons; it is an insulator.

quiz.q: State what is meant by a monomer.
opts: A small molecule that can join with many others to form a polymer
The long chain molecule made when many small molecules join
A single atom that makes up a polymer
A mixture of two different plastics

wrong_explanations.1: The long chain is the POLYMER; the monomer is the small starting molecule.
wrong_explanations.2: A monomer is a molecule, not a single atom.
wrong_explanations.3: A monomer is a pure substance (one type of molecule), not a mixture.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of the double bond, how addition polymerisation forms poly(ethene) from ethene.
opts: The C=C double bond in each ethene opens up; the freed electrons form new bonds to neighbouring monomers, linking them into a long saturated chain
The ethene molecules lose atoms which join to form the chain
The ethene molecules transfer electrons to form ions that attract
Water is removed between each pair of monomers to join them

wrong_explanations.1: In addition polymerisation no atoms are lost — the whole monomer becomes part of the chain.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymerisation of ethene is by opening the double bond (covalent), not by forming ions.
wrong_explanations.3: Removing water describes condensation polymerisation, not the addition polymerisation of ethene.

quiz.q: Explain why a polymer with longer chains tends to have a higher melting point than one with shorter chains.
opts: Longer chains have more points of contact, so the total intermolecular forces between chains are stronger and need more energy to overcome
Longer chains have stronger covalent bonds along them
Longer chains contain ions that attract strongly
Longer chains are always cross-linked

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds along the chain are not what melts; the intermolecular forces between chains are, and these grow with chain length.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymers are molecular, not ionic — there are no ions between the chains.
wrong_explanations.3: Chain length and cross-linking are different things; a longer chain is not automatically cross-linked.

quiz.q: PVC is stiffer and higher-melting than poly(ethene). Suggest why, in terms of intermolecular forces.
opts: PVC contains polar C–Cl bonds, giving stronger intermolecular forces between the chains than in poly(ethene)
PVC has ionic bonds between its chains
PVC has weaker covalent bonds, so it sets harder
PVC chains are metallic and conduct heat away

wrong_explanations.1: PVC is still a molecular polymer, not ionic; the stronger forces come from the polar C–Cl bonds.
wrong_explanations.2: Weaker covalent bonds would make it less stable, not stiffer; the effect is due to stronger intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.3: PVC is an insulator, not metallic.

quiz.q: Explain why melting a polymer does not break the covalent bonds along its chains.
opts: Melting only supplies enough energy to overcome the weak intermolecular forces between the chains, which is far less than needed to break the strong covalent bonds
The covalent bonds are weaker than the forces between chains
The chains gain electrons that protect the bonds
The covalent bonds are shared between the chains

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds are much stronger than the intermolecular forces, so only the weak forces are overcome.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are gained on melting.
wrong_explanations.3: Covalent bonds run along each chain; they are not shared between separate chains.

quiz.q: In addition polymerisation, explain why no small molecule is lost when the monomers join together.
opts: Every atom of each monomer ends up in the polymer chain, because the monomers simply add together at the opened double bond
A water molecule is released, but it is too small to notice
The monomers lose hydrogen atoms as they join
Half of each monomer is left behind as waste

wrong_explanations.1: Losing water is condensation polymerisation; addition polymerisation loses nothing.
wrong_explanations.2: No hydrogen atoms are lost in addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: No part of the monomer is left behind — all atoms are kept in the polymer.

quiz.q: Describe the repeating unit of poly(ethene) and explain how it relates to the ethene monomer.
opts: The repeating unit is —CH₂—CH₂—, written as [—CH₂—CH₂—]ₙ; it contains the same atoms as ethene but the double bond has become a single bond as the monomers joined
The repeating unit is CH₂=CH₂, the same as the monomer including its double bond
The repeating unit is a single carbon atom
The repeating unit contains an extra oxygen atom from the reaction

wrong_explanations.1: In the polymer the C=C double bond has opened to single bonds, so the repeating unit is not still CH₂=CH₂.
wrong_explanations.2: The repeating unit contains two carbons (—CH₂—CH₂—), not a single carbon atom.
wrong_explanations.3: Addition polymerisation adds no atoms; there is no extra oxygen.

quiz.q: Explain why polymers are described as having a simple molecular structure, even though the molecules are very large.
opts: Each polymer chain is a separate molecule, and the chains are held to one another by weak intermolecular forces — the same kind of arrangement as other molecular substances
Because the chains are joined by a giant network of covalent bonds
Because the chains are held together by ionic bonds
Because the chains contain delocalised electrons

wrong_explanations.1: If covalent bonds ran between all the chains it would be a giant covalent structure, not molecular — polymers are held by weak intermolecular forces instead.
wrong_explanations.2: There are no ionic bonds between polymer chains.
wrong_explanations.3: Polymers have no delocalised electrons.

higher.common_mistake: Students often call polymers giant covalent structures because the chains are so large. They are not: a polymer is a very large molecule, and the chains are held to each other only by weak intermolecular forces. In a giant covalent structure such as diamond, strong covalent bonds run all the way through — which is why diamond's melting point is far higher than any polymer's.

bonding/giant-covalent-structures.summary: Describe the structures of diamond and graphite and explain their contrasting properties.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite conducts electricity but diamond does not.
opts: Graphite has a delocalised electron per carbon free to move, while diamond uses all four outer electrons in bonds
Graphite packs more carbon atoms into each layer, and more atoms always means better electrical conductivity
Diamond is transparent, so charge passes through it as light rather than as an electric current
Graphite conducts because it has metallic bonding, and diamond has ionic bonding that traps its electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Conductivity depends on having charge carriers free to move, not on how many atoms the structure contains.
wrong_explanations.2: Whether a material is transparent to light is unrelated to whether it conducts electricity.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent, not metallic or ionic; the free electrons in graphite come from each carbon bonding to only three others.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite is used as a lubricant.
opts: Its atoms form layers held by weak forces between them, so the layers slide over each other easily
Its surface is so perfectly smooth that there are no atoms on it for other surfaces to catch on
It dissolves in water between the surfaces to form a thin, slippery lubricating solution
It is as hard as diamond, and very hard materials naturally make surfaces slippery

wrong_explanations.1: All solids have atoms at their surface; graphite is slippery because its layers slide, not because atoms are absent.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphite does not dissolve in water — it works as a dry lubricant, so no solution is formed.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphite is soft, not hard like diamond; hardness would make it abrasive, not slippery.

quiz.q: Explain why diamond is very hard and has a very high melting point.
opts: Every carbon is joined to four others by strong covalent bonds throughout a giant rigid lattice
Its separate molecules are held to each other by unusually strong intermolecular forces that need lots of energy
It is held together throughout by strong ionic bonds between oppositely charged carbon ions
Its delocalised electrons form a strong sea that holds all the carbon atoms tightly in place

wrong_explanations.1: Diamond is not made of separate molecules — it is one giant covalent lattice, so there are no intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent and contains no ions; there are no ionic bonds to break.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond has no delocalised electrons — all four outer electrons of each carbon are held in covalent bonds.

quiz.q: Describe what a giant covalent structure is.
opts: A structure in which many atoms are all joined by covalent bonds running throughout
A structure built from many small, separate molecules held close together in a regular pattern
A regular lattice of positive and negative ions held together by strong electrostatic attraction
A lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons

wrong_explanations.1: That describes a simple molecular substance; a giant covalent structure has no separate molecules — the bonding is continuous.
wrong_explanations.2: That is a giant ionic lattice; giant covalent structures contain no ions, only atoms joined by covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: That is a giant metallic structure; giant covalent structures have no delocalised electrons or metal ions.

quiz.q: State why diamond and graphite can both be made of carbon yet have very different properties.
opts: They are different forms (allotropes) of carbon, with the atoms joined in different arrangements
They actually contain different elements, which is what gives them their different physical properties
One is a metal and the other a non-metal, so their bonding and properties differ
Diamond is built from ions while graphite is built from small carbon molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Both are pure carbon — the same single element; only the arrangement of the atoms differs.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon is a non-metal in both forms; neither diamond nor graphite is a metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent structures of carbon atoms — neither contains ions or separate small molecules.

quiz.q: Describe the structure of graphene and give one of its uses.
opts: A single layer of graphite, one atom thick, that conducts electricity; used in electronics
A hollow ball of carbon atoms that is soft and slippery; used as a dry lubricant
A giant 3D lattice with four bonds per carbon that is very hard; used in cutting tools
A long chain of many repeating carbon units, joined end to end; used to make plastic bags

wrong_explanations.1: A hollow ball of carbon that acts as a lubricant describes a fullerene, not graphene.
wrong_explanations.2: A hard giant 3D lattice with four bonds per carbon describes diamond, not graphene.
wrong_explanations.3: A long chain of repeating units used for plastic bags describes a polymer, not graphene.

quiz.q: Describe the structure of buckminsterfullerene (C₆₀) and give one of its uses.
opts: A hollow cage of 60 carbon atoms; used to deliver drugs into the body
A single flat sheet of carbon, one atom thick, that conducts; used in electronics
A giant 3D lattice of carbon that is extremely hard; used in cutting tools
A lattice of positive carbon ions in a sea of electrons; used in electrical wiring

wrong_explanations.1: A single flat sheet one atom thick describes graphene, not a fullerene.
wrong_explanations.2: A hard giant 3D lattice used in cutting tools describes diamond, not a hollow fullerene molecule.
wrong_explanations.3: Positive ions in a sea of electrons describes a metal; a fullerene is a covalent carbon molecule.

quiz.q: Describe carbon nanotubes and give one of their uses.
opts: Cylinders of carbon atoms, like rolled-up graphene; very strong, so used to reinforce materials
Hollow balls of carbon atoms that release lots of heat; burned as a fuel for energy
A soft, slippery form of carbon whose layers rub off easily; used to write in pencils
A giant 3D lattice of carbon that is transparent and very hard; used as a gemstone

wrong_explanations.1: Hollow balls of carbon describe fullerenes, and carbon nanostructures are not burned as a fuel.
wrong_explanations.2: A soft, slippery carbon used to write in pencils describes graphite, not nanotubes.
wrong_explanations.3: A hard, transparent giant 3D lattice used as a gemstone describes diamond, not a nanotube.

quiz.q: Identify a use of diamond that depends on its hardness.
opts: Cutting and drilling through hard materials
Electrodes that carry an electric current
Dry lubricant for sliding machine parts
Rubbing off to write on paper

wrong_explanations.1: Carrying current needs a conductor, but diamond has no free electrons and does not conduct.
wrong_explanations.2: A lubricant must be soft and slippery; diamond is the hardest material, so it cannot lubricate.
wrong_explanations.3: Writing needs soft layers that rub off; diamond is too hard to leave a mark this way.

quiz.q: Identify which substance is a giant covalent structure.
opts: Diamond
Sodium chloride
Copper
Carbon dioxide

wrong_explanations.1: Sodium chloride is a giant ionic lattice.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is a metal (giant metallic structure).
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon dioxide is a simple molecular substance, not giant covalent.

quiz.q: State whether diamond conducts electricity, and give a reason.
opts: No — all four outer electrons of each carbon are used in bonds, so none are free to move
Yes — like graphite, each of its carbons has a delocalised outer electron that is free to move
Yes — it is made of carbon, and every form of carbon conducts electricity like graphite does
Only when molten — its carbon ions become free to move once the solid lattice melts

wrong_explanations.1: In diamond every outer electron is held in a covalent bond — unlike graphite, there is no delocalised electron, so it cannot conduct.
wrong_explanations.2: Being made of carbon does not guarantee conduction — graphite conducts but diamond does not, because of how the electrons are used.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond has no ions and no free electrons, so it does not conduct even when molten.

quiz.q: Name the giant covalent form of carbon used for pencil 'lead' and as electrodes.
opts: Graphite
Diamond
Buckminsterfullerene
Poly(ethene)

wrong_explanations.1: Diamond is too hard and does not conduct, so it is not used for pencils or electrodes.
wrong_explanations.2: Buckminsterfullerene is a molecular cage, not the material used for pencil 'lead'.
wrong_explanations.3: Poly(ethene) is a polymer, not a form of carbon used as electrodes.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often assume that because diamond and graphite are both giant covalent structures made of carbon, they must have the same properties. They do not: graphite conducts electricity and is soft and slippery, while diamond does not conduct and is extremely hard. The difference is that in graphite each carbon bonds to only three others, leaving one delocalised electron per atom and weak forces between layers; in diamond every carbon bonds to four others with no free electrons.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite conducts electricity but diamond does not.
opts: Graphite has a delocalised electron per carbon free to move, while diamond uses all four outer electrons in bonds
Graphite packs more carbon atoms into each layer, and more atoms always means better electrical conductivity
Diamond is transparent, so charge passes through it as light rather than as an electric current
Graphite conducts because it has metallic bonding, and diamond has ionic bonding that traps its electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Conductivity depends on having charge carriers free to move, not on how many atoms the structure contains.
wrong_explanations.2: Whether a material is transparent to light is unrelated to whether it conducts electricity.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent, not metallic or ionic; the free electrons in graphite come from each carbon bonding to only three others.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite is used as a lubricant.
opts: Its atoms form layers held by weak forces between them, so the layers slide over each other easily
Its surface is so perfectly smooth that there are no atoms on it for other surfaces to catch on
It dissolves in water between the surfaces to form a thin, slippery lubricating solution
It is as hard as diamond, and very hard materials naturally make surfaces slippery

wrong_explanations.1: All solids have atoms at their surface; graphite is slippery because its layers slide, not because atoms are absent.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphite does not dissolve in water — it works as a dry lubricant, so no solution is formed.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphite is soft, not hard like diamond; hardness would make it abrasive, not slippery.

quiz.q: Explain why diamond is very hard and has a very high melting point.
opts: Every carbon is joined to four others by strong covalent bonds throughout a giant rigid lattice
Its separate molecules are held to each other by unusually strong intermolecular forces that need lots of energy
It is held together throughout by strong ionic bonds between oppositely charged carbon ions
Its delocalised electrons form a strong sea that holds all the carbon atoms tightly in place

wrong_explanations.1: Diamond is not made of separate molecules — it is one giant covalent lattice, so there are no intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent and contains no ions; there are no ionic bonds to break.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond has no delocalised electrons — all four outer electrons of each carbon are held in covalent bonds.

quiz.q: Describe what a giant covalent structure is.
opts: A structure in which many atoms are all joined by covalent bonds running throughout
A structure built from many small, separate molecules held close together in a regular pattern
A regular lattice of positive and negative ions held together by strong electrostatic attraction
A lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons

wrong_explanations.1: That describes a simple molecular substance; a giant covalent structure has no separate molecules — the bonding is continuous.
wrong_explanations.2: That is a giant ionic lattice; giant covalent structures contain no ions, only atoms joined by covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: That is a giant metallic structure; giant covalent structures have no delocalised electrons or metal ions.

quiz.q: State why diamond and graphite can both be made of carbon yet have very different properties.
opts: They are different forms (allotropes) of carbon, with the atoms joined in different arrangements
They actually contain different elements, which is what gives them their different physical properties
One is a metal and the other a non-metal, so their bonding and properties differ
Diamond is built from ions while graphite is built from small carbon molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Both are pure carbon — the same single element; only the arrangement of the atoms differs.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon is a non-metal in both forms; neither diamond nor graphite is a metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent structures of carbon atoms — neither contains ions or separate small molecules.

quiz.q: Explain why silicon dioxide (found in sand and quartz) has a very high melting point.
opts: It is giant covalent, so many strong Si–O covalent bonds must be broken to melt it
It is ionic, so strong electrostatic forces between Si and O ions must be overcome
It is a simple molecule, so strong intermolecular forces between SiO₂ molecules must be broken
It is a metal, so a strong sea of delocalised electrons holds the ions together

wrong_explanations.1: Silicon dioxide is giant covalent, not ionic — it contains no ions, only Si and O atoms joined by covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.2: It is not simple molecular — the Si–O covalent bonds run throughout the whole structure, with no separate molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: Silicon dioxide is a non-metal compound with no delocalised electrons; its bonding is covalent, not metallic.

quiz.q: Graphene is a single layer of graphite. Explain why it conducts electricity and is very strong.
opts: Each carbon covalently bonds to three others in a strong network, and its fourth outer electron is delocalised
It contains free-moving ions that carry charge, and the ionic bonds between them make it strong
It is a metal, so its sea of delocalised electrons lets it conduct and hold together strongly
Its molecules are held to each other by strong intermolecular forces, like a simple molecular solid

wrong_explanations.1: Graphene has no ions — its conduction comes from delocalised electrons, and its strength from covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphene is a covalent form of carbon, not a metal, though it does conduct via delocalised electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphene is one giant covalent sheet, not separate molecules, so there are no intermolecular forces.

quiz.q: Explain why buckminsterfullerene (C₆₀) can be used to deliver drugs to specific parts of the body.
opts: Its molecules are hollow cages, so a drug can be carried inside and released where needed
It is a metal, so the drug is held on its surface by a sea of delocalised electrons
It reacts with the drug to form a new compound that then dissolves and spreads through the blood
It is giant covalent, so its bonds run throughout and hold the drug tightly inside the structure

wrong_explanations.1: C₆₀ is a covalent carbon molecule, not a metal, so it has no delocalised-electron surface to carry a drug.
wrong_explanations.2: The drug is held physically inside the hollow cage; C₆₀ does not react with it to carry it.
wrong_explanations.3: C₆₀ is a small hollow molecule, not a giant covalent structure — the drug sits inside the cage, not bonded throughout.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite has a high melting point even though its layers can slide over each other easily.
opts: Melting breaks the strong covalent bonds within layers, while sliding only overcomes the weak forces between layers
The layers are held to each other by strong ionic bonds that need a lot of energy to break
All of its delocalised electrons must first be removed before the layers can break apart and melt
Graphite does not really have a high melting point; it softens easily because the layers slide

wrong_explanations.1: There are no ionic bonds in graphite; the high melting point comes from strong covalent bonds within each layer, not forces between layers.
wrong_explanations.2: Melting breaks covalent bonds within the layers; the delocalised electrons are not removed and are not what must be overcome to melt it.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphite does have a high melting point, because of the strong covalent bonds within each layer.

quiz.q: Compare diamond and graphite in terms of the number of bonds each carbon forms and whether they conduct electricity.
opts: Diamond forms 4 bonds per carbon and does not conduct; graphite forms 3, leaving a delocalised electron, so conducts
Both form 4 bonds per carbon, and both conduct because each has a spare delocalised electron
Both form 3 bonds per carbon, and neither conducts because every electron is used in a bond
Diamond forms 3 bonds and conducts, while graphite forms 4 bonds and does not conduct

wrong_explanations.1: Only graphite conducts, and only diamond forms 4 bonds; diamond has all its electrons in bonds, so no spare delocalised electron.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond forms 4 bonds (not 3) and graphite does conduct, because graphite has a fourth electron that is delocalised.
wrong_explanations.3: This swaps them over: diamond forms 4 bonds and does not conduct; graphite forms 3 and does conduct.

quiz.q: Carbon nanotubes are rolled sheets of graphene. Suggest why they are useful both for reinforcing materials and in electronics.
opts: Their strong covalent network makes them very strong for reinforcing, and delocalised electrons let them conduct
They are soft and flexible, and this softness is what lets them conduct electricity so well
They are ionic, so their free-moving ions make them both strong and able to conduct electricity
They dissolve into a material and bond with it chemically, which strengthens it and carries current

wrong_explanations.1: Nanotubes are strong, not soft; they conduct because of delocalised electrons, and softness would not help conduction.
wrong_explanations.2: Nanotubes are covalent carbon with no ions; conduction is by delocalised electrons, not free-moving ions.
wrong_explanations.3: Nanotubes do not dissolve or bond chemically into materials; their strength comes from their covalent structure.

quiz.q: Explain why graphene is described as both extremely strong and an excellent electrical conductor, in terms of its bonding.
opts: Each carbon bonds covalently to three others in a strong 2D network, and its fourth outer electron delocalises across the sheet
It has strong ionic bonds giving strength, and free-moving ions that let it conduct across the sheet
It is a metal with a giant metallic lattice, so its sea of delocalised electrons gives strength and conductivity
Strong intermolecular forces hold its molecules together and also let electrons flow freely across the sheet

wrong_explanations.1: Graphene has no ions or ionic bonds; its strength comes from covalent bonds and its conductivity from delocalised electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphene is a covalent form of carbon, not a metal, though its delocalised electrons do let it conduct.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphene is one covalent sheet, not separate molecules; there are no intermolecular forces, and conduction is by delocalised electrons.

higher.common_mistake: Students often assume that because diamond and graphite are both giant covalent structures made of carbon, they must have the same properties. They do not: graphite conducts electricity and is soft and slippery, while diamond does not conduct and is extremely hard. The difference is that in graphite each carbon bonds to only three others, leaving one delocalised electron per atom and weak forces between layers; in diamond every carbon bonds to four others with no free electrons.

bonding/metals-alloys.summary: Explain the properties of metals and alloys in terms of metallic bonding and structure.

quiz.q: Explain why metals can conduct electricity in the solid state, but ionic compounds cannot.
opts: Metals have delocalised electrons that are free to move even when solid; in an ionic solid the ions are locked in fixed positions and cannot move
Metal atoms are smaller than ions, so they move more easily through the solid
Metals have more protons, which makes them better at conducting
Ionic bonds are stronger, which stops any particle from moving

wrong_explanations.1: Atom or ion size is not the reason — it is the availability of free-moving charge carriers.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton number does not determine conductivity; many non-metals have lots of protons but do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds CAN conduct when molten (ions freed); the issue in the solid is fixed ions, not stronger bonds.

quiz.q: Explain why brass (copper mixed with zinc) is harder than pure copper.
opts: The zinc atoms are a different size to the copper atoms, so they distort the regular lattice and stop the layers of atoms sliding over each other
Brass has more metallic bonds than copper, and more bonds means harder
Zinc is a harder metal, so mixing it in makes the product hard
Brass has been purified, and pure metals are always harder

wrong_explanations.1: More bonds does not necessarily mean harder — the hardening is due to lattice disruption preventing layers sliding.
wrong_explanations.2: The key mechanism is disrupting the regular copper lattice with differently-sized atoms, not zinc's own hardness.
wrong_explanations.3: Brass is a mixture (less pure than copper); it is the added, differently-sized atoms that harden it.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are good conductors of heat.
opts: The delocalised electrons are free to move and quickly transfer thermal energy through the structure
The positive ions carry the heat as they move around
Metals contain water that spreads the heat
The covalent bonds pass the heat along

wrong_explanations.1: The positive ions are fixed and only vibrate; the mobile delocalised electrons carry most of the heat.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals do not contain water; heat is carried by the delocalised electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals have no covalent bonds; heat is transferred by the electron sea.

quiz.q: Describe the difference in structure between a pure metal and one of its alloys.
opts: A pure metal has a regular lattice of same-sized atoms; an alloy has atoms of different sizes that distort the regular arrangement
A pure metal contains ions but an alloy contains only atoms
A pure metal is molecular but an alloy is a giant lattice
A pure metal has no bonding but an alloy has metallic bonding

wrong_explanations.1: Both have positive ions and a sea of electrons; the difference is that the alloy has differently-sized atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Both are giant metallic structures, not molecular.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are held by metallic bonding; the difference is the mix of atom sizes.

quiz.q: Explain why pure metals are often too soft for use, so alloys are made instead.
opts: In a pure metal the layers of same-sized atoms can slide over each other easily, making it soft; alloying adds differently-sized atoms that stop the layers sliding
Pure metals contain no metallic bonds, so they fall apart
Pure metals are always liquids at room temperature
Pure metals have too many delocalised electrons to be strong

wrong_explanations.1: Pure metals do have metallic bonding; they are soft because their identical-sized atoms let layers slide.
wrong_explanations.2: Most pure metals are solid at room temperature; they are simply soft.
wrong_explanations.3: The number of delocalised electrons is not the reason; sliding layers of identical atoms is.

quiz.q: Name the type of structure that a metal has.
opts: A giant metallic structure (lattice)
A simple molecular structure
A giant ionic lattice
A single small molecule

wrong_explanations.1: Metals are giant metallic structures, not made of small molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: A giant ionic lattice has positive and negative ions; a metal has positive ions and an electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: A metal is a giant structure, not one small molecule.

quiz.q: State what an alloy is.
opts: A mixture of a metal with one or more other elements
A very pure sample of a single metal
A compound of a metal and a non-metal
A metal that has had all its electrons removed

wrong_explanations.1: A pure single metal is the opposite of an alloy.
wrong_explanations.2: An alloy is a mixture, not a chemically bonded compound.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals in an alloy keep their delocalised electrons.

quiz.q: Identify a use of copper that depends on it being a good electrical conductor.
opts: Electrical wiring
Making window glass
As a fuel for burning
As a fertiliser

wrong_explanations.1: Window glass is not a metal and is not chosen for conductivity.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is not used as a fuel.
wrong_explanations.3: Copper is not used as a fertiliser; wiring uses its high conductivity.

quiz.q: Identify the correct description of steel.
opts: Iron mixed with a small amount of carbon
Pure iron with nothing added
Copper mixed with zinc
A non-metal element

wrong_explanations.1: Steel is an ALLOY — pure iron with nothing added is not steel.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper mixed with zinc is brass, not steel.
wrong_explanations.3: Steel is a metal alloy, not a non-metal element.

quiz.q: State whether metals are malleable or brittle.
opts: Malleable — they can be hammered into shape
Brittle — they shatter when struck
Neither — they are liquids
Both equally

wrong_explanations.1: Brittleness is a property of ionic compounds; metals bend rather than shatter.
wrong_explanations.2: Most metals are solid at room temperature, not liquid.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals are clearly malleable, not brittle.

quiz.q: Identify the property that makes copper suitable for the base of a saucepan.
opts: It is a good conductor of heat
It is a good electrical insulator
It has a very low melting point
It is brittle

wrong_explanations.1: An insulator would be poor for a saucepan base; copper is chosen for good heat conduction.
wrong_explanations.2: A very low melting point would be dangerous in cooking.
wrong_explanations.3: Brittleness would make the pan shatter; copper is malleable and conducts heat well.

quiz.q: Name the two metals mixed together to make bronze.
opts: Copper and tin
Copper and zinc
Iron and carbon
Iron and chromium

wrong_explanations.1: Copper and zinc make brass, not bronze.
wrong_explanations.2: Iron and carbon make steel.
wrong_explanations.3: Iron and chromium (with nickel) make stainless steel.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often think that because ionic compounds only conduct when molten, metals must be the same. They are not: a metal conducts in the solid state because its delocalised electrons are already free to move. Ionic compounds cannot conduct as solids because their charge carriers are ions, which stay locked in place until the compound melts or dissolves.

quiz.q: Explain why metals can conduct electricity in the solid state, but ionic compounds cannot.
opts: Metals have delocalised electrons that are free to move even when solid; in an ionic solid the ions are locked in fixed positions and cannot move
Metal atoms are smaller than ions, so they move more easily through the solid
Metals have more protons, which makes them better at conducting
Ionic bonds are stronger, which stops any particle from moving

wrong_explanations.1: Atom or ion size is not the reason — it is the availability of free-moving charge carriers.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton number does not determine conductivity; many non-metals have lots of protons but do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds CAN conduct when molten (ions freed); the issue in the solid is fixed ions, not stronger bonds.

quiz.q: Explain why brass (copper mixed with zinc) is harder than pure copper.
opts: The zinc atoms are a different size to the copper atoms, so they distort the regular lattice and stop the layers of atoms sliding over each other
Brass has more metallic bonds than copper, and more bonds means harder
Zinc is a harder metal, so mixing it in makes the product hard
Brass has been purified, and pure metals are always harder

wrong_explanations.1: More bonds does not necessarily mean harder — the hardening is due to lattice disruption preventing layers sliding.
wrong_explanations.2: The key mechanism is disrupting the regular copper lattice with differently-sized atoms, not zinc's own hardness.
wrong_explanations.3: Brass is a mixture (less pure than copper); it is the added, differently-sized atoms that harden it.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are good conductors of heat.
opts: The delocalised electrons are free to move and quickly transfer thermal energy through the structure
The positive ions carry the heat as they move around
Metals contain water that spreads the heat
The covalent bonds pass the heat along

wrong_explanations.1: The positive ions are fixed and only vibrate; the mobile delocalised electrons carry most of the heat.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals do not contain water; heat is carried by the delocalised electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals have no covalent bonds; heat is transferred by the electron sea.

quiz.q: Describe the difference in structure between a pure metal and one of its alloys.
opts: A pure metal has a regular lattice of same-sized atoms; an alloy has atoms of different sizes that distort the regular arrangement
A pure metal contains ions but an alloy contains only atoms
A pure metal is molecular but an alloy is a giant lattice
A pure metal has no bonding but an alloy has metallic bonding

wrong_explanations.1: Both have positive ions and a sea of electrons; the difference is that the alloy has differently-sized atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Both are giant metallic structures, not molecular.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are held by metallic bonding; the difference is the mix of atom sizes.

quiz.q: Explain why pure metals are often too soft for use, so alloys are made instead.
opts: In a pure metal the layers of same-sized atoms can slide over each other easily, making it soft; alloying adds differently-sized atoms that stop the layers sliding
Pure metals contain no metallic bonds, so they fall apart
Pure metals are always liquids at room temperature
Pure metals have too many delocalised electrons to be strong

wrong_explanations.1: Pure metals do have metallic bonding; they are soft because their identical-sized atoms let layers slide.
wrong_explanations.2: Most pure metals are solid at room temperature; they are simply soft.
wrong_explanations.3: The number of delocalised electrons is not the reason; sliding layers of identical atoms is.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of particles, why metals conduct electricity and heat but simple molecular substances do not.
opts: Metals contain delocalised electrons that are free to move and carry charge and thermal energy; simple molecular substances have no free electrons or ions
Metals contain free ions but molecules do not
Metals have stronger covalent bonds than molecules
Simple molecular substances have delocalised electrons that are fixed

wrong_explanations.1: Metals conduct via delocalised electrons, not free ions (ions are fixed in a solid metal).
wrong_explanations.2: Metals have no covalent bonds; conduction is due to the electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances have no delocalised electrons at all.

quiz.q: Stainless steel is used to make cutlery. Suggest two properties that make it suitable and relate them to its structure.
opts: It is hard and strong (differently-sized atoms disrupt the lattice so layers cannot slide) and it resists corrosion
It is soft and brittle, so it can be shaped and snapped
It has a low melting point, so it melts clean
It is a good insulator, so it is safe to hold

wrong_explanations.1: Soft, brittle cutlery would be useless; stainless steel is hard and strong because of its alloy structure.
wrong_explanations.2: A low melting point would be a disadvantage for cutlery.
wrong_explanations.3: Being a conductor or insulator is not the key reason cutlery is made from stainless steel.

quiz.q: Explain why an alloy is generally harder than the pure metal it is made from, using the idea of layers of atoms.
opts: The different-sized atoms in the alloy distort the regular layers, so the layers can no longer slide over each other easily
The alloy has weaker metallic bonds, which makes it more rigid
The alloy contains ionic bonds that hold it firmly
The alloy has fewer delocalised electrons, so it is stronger

wrong_explanations.1: Weaker bonds would make it softer, not harder; the hardening is due to lattice distortion.
wrong_explanations.2: Alloys are still held by metallic bonding, not ionic bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: The number of delocalised electrons is not the reason; disrupting the layers is.

quiz.q: Aluminium is used for aircraft bodies. Suggest why an aluminium alloy is used rather than pure aluminium.
opts: The alloy is much stronger and harder than pure aluminium while still having a low density, so the aircraft is strong but light
Pure aluminium is a better conductor, which is dangerous in flight
The alloy is cheaper because it contains no aluminium
Pure aluminium is a gas at room temperature

wrong_explanations.1: Conductivity is not the reason; the alloy is chosen because it is stronger while remaining light.
wrong_explanations.2: Aluminium alloys still contain mostly aluminium.
wrong_explanations.3: Aluminium is a solid metal at room temperature.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are malleable but ionic compounds are brittle, in terms of what happens when the layers move.
opts: In a metal the layers of ions slide and the electron sea keeps them bonded, so it bends; in an ionic solid, shifting the layers brings like charges together, which repel and split the crystal
Both bend, but metals bend more quickly
Metals contain covalent bonds that flex; ionic compounds do not
Ionic compounds contain delocalised electrons that snap

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds do not bend at all — they shatter, because of ion repulsion when layers shift.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals have no covalent bonds; malleability comes from the sliding layers held by the electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds have no delocalised electrons; that is a metallic feature.

quiz.q: Explain why steel is more useful than pure iron for constructing buildings and bridges, linking its hardness to the arrangement of its atoms.
opts: The carbon atoms in steel are a different size to the iron atoms, disrupting the regular lattice so the layers cannot slide; this makes steel much harder and stronger, so it can bear heavy loads
Steel is purer than iron, and pure metals bear more load
Steel contains ionic bonds that make it rigid
Steel is lighter than iron because it contains carbon

wrong_explanations.1: Steel is LESS pure than iron (carbon added); it is the differently-sized carbon atoms disrupting the lattice that harden it.
wrong_explanations.2: Steel is held by metallic bonding, not ionic bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: The reason steel is chosen is its hardness and strength from lattice disruption, not a large weight saving.

quiz.q: Explain why a metal's electrical conductivity and its thermal conductivity both depend on the same feature of its structure.
opts: Both rely on the delocalised electrons: they move to carry electric charge and also transfer thermal energy quickly through the structure
Both rely on the positive ions moving through the metal
Both rely on covalent bonds passing energy along
Both rely on the metal being an ionic lattice

wrong_explanations.1: The positive ions are fixed and only vibrate; it is the delocalised electrons that carry both charge and heat.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals have no covalent bonds; the electron sea carries both current and heat.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals are metallic, not ionic, structures.

higher.common_mistake: Students often think that because ionic compounds only conduct when molten, metals must be the same. They are not: a metal conducts in the solid state because its delocalised electrons are already free to move. Ionic compounds cannot conduct as solids because their charge carriers are ions, which stay locked in place until the compound melts or dissolves.

bonding/nanoparticles.summary: Describe nanoparticles and fullerenes, their properties and uses, and potential risks.

quiz.q: Explain why nanoparticles have different properties from the same material in bulk (large-scale) form.
opts: They have a much larger surface-area-to-volume ratio, so a greater fraction of their atoms are on the surface and available to take part in reactions
They are made of different atoms from the bulk material
They have weaker chemical bonds than the bulk material
They are always more stable than the bulk material

wrong_explanations.1: Nanoparticles are made of the SAME atoms as the bulk material — it is the scale that changes the properties.
wrong_explanations.2: The bonds are the same type as in the bulk; what differs is the fraction of atoms on the surface.
wrong_explanations.3: Nanoparticles are often MORE reactive (so less stable in some ways), not simply more stable.

quiz.q: Suggest why nanoparticles may pose a greater health risk than the same material in larger particle form.
opts: Their very small size may let them pass through cell membranes and into cells, and their long-term effects are not yet fully understood
They are more toxic because they contain more of the harmful material
They are heavier, so they stay in the lungs longer
They react with water in the body to make dangerous chemicals

wrong_explanations.1: A nanoparticle contains LESS material than a larger particle — the concern is its size and reactivity, not the amount.
wrong_explanations.2: Nanoparticles are lighter than larger particles; the concern is that their small size lets them penetrate the body.
wrong_explanations.3: The main concern is their ability to penetrate cells because of their size, not a specific reaction with water.

quiz.q: Describe what happens to a particle's surface-area-to-volume ratio as the particle gets smaller.
opts: The ratio increases — smaller particles have a greater surface area compared with their volume
The ratio decreases as the particle gets smaller
The ratio stays the same whatever the size
The ratio becomes zero for very small particles

wrong_explanations.1: Smaller particles have a HIGHER surface-area-to-volume ratio, not a lower one.
wrong_explanations.2: The ratio definitely changes with size — it rises as particles get smaller.
wrong_explanations.3: The ratio actually becomes very large (not zero) for very small particles.

quiz.q: Silver nanoparticles are added to wound dressings. Suggest why their small size makes them suitable for this use.
opts: Their large surface-area-to-volume ratio makes them very reactive, so they are strongly antibacterial and only a small amount is needed
Their large size lets them cover the whole wound
They are cheaper than any other material
They dissolve completely to form a harmless gas

wrong_explanations.1: Nanoparticles are small, not large; it is their high surface-area-to-volume ratio that makes them effective.
wrong_explanations.2: Cost is not the property being asked about; their antibacterial action comes from their high reactivity.
wrong_explanations.3: They do not simply dissolve into a gas; their antibacterial effect is due to their reactive surface.

quiz.q: Explain why nanoparticles make very effective catalysts.
opts: Their high surface-area-to-volume ratio provides a large surface for reactions, so a small mass is very effective
They are used up during the reaction, which speeds it up
They dissolve into the reactants to react faster
They increase the temperature of the reaction

wrong_explanations.1: Catalysts are not used up in a reaction; nanoparticles work because of their large reactive surface.
wrong_explanations.2: A catalyst is not consumed and does not dissolve into the reactants; it provides a surface.
wrong_explanations.3: Catalysts provide an alternative pathway; they do not simply heat the reaction up.

quiz.q: Titanium dioxide nanoparticles are used in sunscreen. Suggest why the nanoparticle form is used instead of bulk titanium dioxide.
opts: At the nanoscale it is transparent on the skin but still absorbs harmful UV radiation, unlike bulk titanium dioxide which is white
The nanoparticle form is a different, safer chemical
The nanoparticle form reflects all light so the skin looks white
The nanoparticle form makes the sunscreen conduct electricity

wrong_explanations.1: It is the same chemical (titanium dioxide) — only the particle size differs, which changes its appearance.
wrong_explanations.2: Bulk TiO₂ is the white one; the nanoparticle form is chosen because it is transparent, not white.
wrong_explanations.3: Conductivity is irrelevant to sunscreen; the point is transparency plus UV absorption.

quiz.q: State the range of sizes (in nanometres) that defines a nanoparticle.
opts: 1 to 100 nm
1 to 100 mm
1 to 100 cm

wrong_explanations.1: Nanoparticles are measured in nanometres (nm), not millimetres — mm are a million times larger.
wrong_explanations.2: Centimetres are far too large; nanoparticles are 1–100 nm.
wrong_explanations.3: Nanoparticles are defined as 1–100 nm, not 100–1000 nm.

quiz.q: Identify a use of nanoparticles.
opts: In sunscreens, as an antibacterial agent, or as a catalyst
As the main structural beams in a building
As a fuel burned in power stations
As window glass

wrong_explanations.1: Structural beams use bulk metals/alloys, not nanoparticles.
wrong_explanations.2: Nanoparticles are not burned as a fuel.
wrong_explanations.3: Window glass is a bulk material, not a nanoparticle application.

quiz.q: Name the carbon nanostructure that is a single layer of graphite, one atom thick.
opts: Graphene
Diamond
Buckminsterfullerene
Poly(ethene)

wrong_explanations.1: Diamond is a 3D giant covalent lattice, not a single layer.
wrong_explanations.2: Buckminsterfullerene is a hollow cage of 60 carbon atoms, not a flat layer.
wrong_explanations.3: Poly(ethene) is a polymer, not a form of carbon.

quiz.q: Identify one possible risk of using nanoparticles.
opts: They may penetrate cells, and their long-term effects are not yet fully understood
They always explode when exposed to air
They make materials much heavier
They can only ever be used once

wrong_explanations.1: Nanoparticles do not routinely explode in air; the concern is their ability to enter cells.
wrong_explanations.2: Nanoparticles are extremely light; they do not make materials heavier.
wrong_explanations.3: Reusability is not the health/environmental risk being described.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often think that a nanoparticle of gold must be a different substance from ordinary gold because its colour and reactivity are so different. It is the same substance — the same gold atoms. What changes is the scale: nanoparticles have a far larger surface-area-to-volume ratio, so a much greater fraction of their atoms sit on the surface, which changes their properties.

fifas.label: Surface area to volume ratio of a cube (side 2)
fifas.question: A cube-shaped particle has sides of length 2. Calculate its surface area to volume ratio.
